questions.question: What is the main benefit of using Temporal Schedules to automate a recurring task, compared to building your own scheduling logic?

choices.text: Temporal Schedules automatically encrypt all workflow data using built-in security policies.
Temporal Schedules let you automate repetitive tasks, reduce manual intervention, and ensure timely execution of business processes through a durable, managed scheduling system.
Temporal Schedules guarantee that each workflow run completes within a fixed time window defined at creation.
Temporal Schedules replace the need for Workers by running workflow logic directly on the Temporal Service.

questions.explanation: Temporal Schedules exist precisely to take the burden of time-based automation off your application code. Instead of writing brittle cron jobs or retry loops, you define a Schedule once and Temporal durably manages when and how workflows fire — including handling missed runs, pausing, and backfilling. This is a core part of Temporal's value: durable execution without custom infrastructure.

questions.question: When you delete a Temporal Schedule, what happens to the Workflow Executions that were already started by that Schedule?

choices.text: All previously started Workflow Executions are immediately cancelled and their results are discarded.
Previously started Workflow Executions are paused until the Schedule is recreated with the same ID.
Deletion does not affect any Workflow Executions that were already started by the Schedule; they continue running normally.
Previously started Workflow Executions are archived and can only be accessed through the describe action.

questions.explanation: Deleting a Schedule only removes the scheduling definition — it does not touch any Workflow Executions that the Schedule already launched. Those workflows live their own independent lifecycle. This makes sense because a Schedule is just a trigger mechanism; the workflows themselves are separate, durable entities managed by Temporal.

questions.question: What does the 'backfill' action on a Temporal Schedule allow you to do?

choices.text: Reset a Schedule's interval so it fires more frequently going forward from the current moment.
Execute workflow actions for a past time range, which is useful for catching up on missed or delayed runs.
Copy an existing Schedule's configuration and apply it to a new Schedule with a different ID.
Archive old Schedule runs so they no longer appear in the list of active schedules.

questions.explanation: Backfilling lets you retroactively run the actions that a Schedule would have fired during a past time window. For example, if your scheduled data-processing workflow was missed for a week, you can backfill that range and Temporal will execute those runs as if they had fired on time. This is much cleaner than manually triggering individual runs or writing one-off scripts.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'pause' action on a Temporal Schedule?

choices.text: It permanently stops the Schedule and removes it from the list of available Schedules.
It temporarily halts all future Workflow Runs on the Schedule without deleting it, so it can be resumed later.
It cancels any currently running Workflow Executions that were started by the Schedule.
It resets the Schedule's next fire time to the current moment so the next run happens immediately.

questions.explanation: Pausing a Schedule is a reversible, non-destructive operation. Future workflow runs stop firing, but the Schedule definition is preserved and can be unpaused at any time. This is handy for maintenance windows or temporary holds — you don't need to delete and recreate the Schedule just to stop it briefly.

questions.question: How does 'Start Delay' differ from a recurring Temporal Schedule?

choices.text: Start Delay fires a workflow on a repeating interval, while a Schedule fires it only once at a specific time.
Start Delay is only available in the Go SDK, while Schedules work across all supported SDKs.
Start Delay runs the workflow immediately but delays sending results back to the caller by the specified duration.
Start Delay triggers a workflow once after a specified wait time, while a Schedule fires workflow executions repeatedly according to a defined spec.

questions.explanation: Start Delay is a one-shot mechanism: you say 'run this workflow, but wait X hours first.' A Schedule, by contrast, defines an ongoing recurring pattern (like every 5 days). Use Start Delay when you need a single future execution; use a Schedule when you need repeated executions over time.

questions.question: Temporal's documentation cautions against using Temporal Cron Jobs and recommends Schedules instead. What is the main reason given for preferring Schedules?

choices.text: Cron Jobs use a different task queue from normal workflows, which adds unnecessary routing complexity.
Schedules offer more configuration options and the ability to update or pause them while running, giving a better developer experience than Cron Jobs.
Cron Jobs cannot be monitored through the Temporal Web UI, making them difficult to debug in production.
Cron Jobs require a separate worker process dedicated solely to scheduling, increasing operational overhead.

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly flags Cron Jobs as not recommended and points to Schedules as the superior replacement. Schedules were purpose-built with richer features: you can inspect them, pause them, backfill missed runs, and update their configuration on the fly — none of which are easily done with the older Cron Job approach. This is a good example of Temporal evolving its primitives to give developers better control over long-running automation.

questions.question: In Go, what is the correct way to trigger a Schedule to run immediately outside its normal cadence?

choices.text: Call `scheduleHandle.Backfill()` with a start time of `now` and an end time of `now + 1 second`.
Call `scheduleHandle.Trigger()`, which by default respects the Schedule's Overlap Policy.
Call `scheduleHandle.Unpause()` to force an immediate execution, then re-pause afterward.
Call `temporalClient.ExecuteWorkflow()` with the same workflow ID used by the Schedule.

questions.explanation: The `Trigger()` method on a `ScheduleHandle` is specifically designed for immediate, out-of-band execution of a scheduled action. By default it honors the Schedule's Overlap Policy, but you can override this via `ScheduleTriggerOptions`. Use this when you need to run a scheduled job right now — for example, to test it or recover from an outage — without waiting for the next scheduled interval. A (Backfill) is for executing missed time ranges in the past, not for immediate runs. C (Unpause) simply resumes the schedule's normal cadence; it doesn't force an immediate run. D bypasses the Schedule entirely and would create an unrelated Workflow Execution.

questions.question: You need to replay three hours of missed nightly ETL jobs that a Schedule failed to fire due to an infrastructure outage. Which Schedule action is most appropriate?

choices.text: Call `Trigger()` three times with `ScheduleOverlapPolicy.AllowAll` to manually run each missed job.
Delete and recreate the Schedule with a past `StartAt` to let Temporal replay the missed firings automatically.
Call `Update()` to set the Schedule's start time to the beginning of the outage, then revert it after the backfill completes.
Call `Backfill()` specifying a start and end time covering the outage window along with an appropriate Overlap Policy.

questions.explanation: Backfill is purpose-built for executing the Actions that a Schedule would have fired during a specific past time range. You provide `StartAt`, `EndAt`, and an Overlap Policy (such as `AllowAll`) and Temporal fires the appropriate number of Workflow Executions as if the Schedule had run normally during that window. This is exactly what you'd reach for after an outage. A (Trigger) is for a single immediate run; doing it three times manually is error-prone and doesn't tie the runs to their logical scheduled times. B (Delete/recreate) is destructive and incorrect — Temporal doesn't auto-replay history on creation. C (Update) changes the ongoing schedule configuration, not historical execution.

questions.question: In the .NET SDK, what does `ListSchedulesAsync()` return, and what consistency guarantee does it provide?

choices.text: A strongly consistent snapshot list of all Schedules at the moment of the call, returned as a `Task<IList<ScheduleListDescription>>`.
An async enumerable of Schedule descriptions; recently added or deleted Schedules may not appear immediately due to eventual consistency.
A paginated cursor of Schedule IDs only (not descriptions), with strong read-after-write consistency guarantees.
A synchronous `IEnumerable` of Schedules filtered to only those created by the current client connection.

questions.explanation: `ListSchedulesAsync()` returns an async enumerable (iterated with `await foreach`), and the documentation explicitly notes that if a schedule is added or deleted, it may not be available in the list immediately. This is because Schedule visibility is backed by an eventually consistent visibility store. Operators running Temporal in production should account for this lag when building dashboards or automation that reacts to schedule changes. A is wrong because the list is not strongly consistent. C is wrong because descriptions are included and there is no read-after-write guarantee. D is wrong because the return is async, not synchronous, and there is no filtering by client identity.

questions.question: What is the key difference between using `StartDelay` on `WorkflowOptions` versus creating a one-time Schedule to start a Workflow in the future?

choices.text: `StartDelay` queues the Workflow in the Task Queue immediately but the Worker ignores it until the delay elapses, while a Schedule fires the Workflow at the specified time from outside the Worker.

`StartDelay` is a simple option on the workflow start call that defers execution by a fixed duration without creating a separate Schedule entity, while a Schedule creates a managed, observable, and updatable entity in the Temporal Service.
`StartDelay` creates a hidden internal Schedule under the hood, while using the Schedule API creates a user-visible Schedule that appears in `ListSchedules`.
`StartDelay` supports recurring executions with a minimum interval of one hour, while Schedules are used exclusively for one-time future executions.

questions.explanation: `StartDelay` is a lightweight option you set on `StartWorkflowOptions` (or `WorkflowOptions` in .NET) to delay a single Workflow Execution by a fixed duration. It doesn't create a Schedule entity — there's nothing to list, pause, update, or delete. A Schedule, on the other hand, is a first-class Temporal entity with its own ID, spec, overlap policy, pause/trigger/backfill controls, and visibility in `ListSchedules`. Reach for `StartDelay` when you just need a one-off delayed start; reach for Schedules when you need recurring execution, observability, or runtime control. A incorrectly describes Task Queue mechanics. C is wrong — `StartDelay` does not create a hidden Schedule. D has the use cases exactly backwards.

questions.question: When does the Temporal Service automatically delete a Schedule, according to the documentation?

choices.text: After the Schedule has been paused for more than 30 days without being unpaused.
Immediately after the last scheduled Workflow Execution it fires reaches a terminal state (Completed, Failed, or Timed Out).
Once the Schedule has finished creating all its Workflow Executions and will not fire again, though the exact timing is not guaranteed.
Schedules are never automatically deleted; they must always be explicitly removed with the Delete action.

questions.explanation: The documentation describes a 'Schedule Auto-Deletion' behavior: once a Schedule has completed creating all of its Workflow Executions (i.e., it won't fire again — for example, a one-time Schedule whose time has passed), the Temporal Service will delete it automatically. However, the Service does not guarantee exactly when this cleanup will occur. This matters in practice because you shouldn't rely on the Schedule being present for inspection immediately after its last firing. A (pause-based deletion) is not described anywhere. B incorrectly ties deletion to the Workflow Execution's terminal state rather than the Schedule's firing cycle. D is incorrect because auto-deletion does occur for fully exhausted Schedules.

questions.question: In Go, how does the `Update()` method on a ScheduleHandle receive the current Schedule configuration when you need to modify it?

choices.text: You call `Describe()` first to get the current Schedule, mutate it, then pass the mutated struct directly to `Update()`.
`Update()` accepts a callback function that receives a `ScheduleUpdateInput` containing the current description, and returns a `*ScheduleUpdate` with the desired state.
`Update()` accepts a full `ScheduleOptions` struct that completely replaces the existing Schedule configuration with no access to the current state.
`Update()` accepts a partial patch struct where only the fields you set are changed and unset fields retain their current values automatically.

questions.explanation: The Go SDK's `Update()` uses an optimistic-concurrency callback pattern: you provide a function (`DoUpdate`) that receives `ScheduleUpdateInput`, which contains the current schedule `Description`. Inside the callback you can read the current state and return a `*ScheduleUpdate` containing the new Schedule. This prevents race conditions where two concurrent updates could overwrite each other blindly. The .NET SDK uses the same pattern with a lambda. A is wrong because calling `Describe()` separately is not how the official update pattern works — the callback receives the state atomically. C is wrong because the callback-based approach does provide access to current state. D is wrong because there is no partial-patch mechanism; you return a complete Schedule.

questions.question: The documentation recommends using Schedules instead of Temporal Cron Jobs. Which of the following best describes the practical limitation of Cron Jobs compared to Schedules?

choices.text: Cron Jobs only support hourly or daily intervals, while Schedules support arbitrary cron expressions and interval-based specs.
Cron Jobs cannot be paused, triggered manually, backfilled, or updated at runtime without stopping and restarting the Workflow, while Schedules support all of these operations.
Cron Jobs require a separate Temporal Worker dedicated to scheduling, increasing infrastructure complexity versus Schedules.
Cron Jobs use the Server's system clock for timing, which is less reliable than the dedicated scheduling service backing Schedules.

questions.explanation: The documentation flags Cron Jobs as not recommended and explicitly states that Schedules 'provide a better developer experience, including more configuration options and the ability to update or pause running Schedules.' In practice, Cron Jobs are just a `CronSchedule` string baked into the Workflow start options — they lack the rich lifecycle operations (pause, unpause, trigger, backfill, update, describe, list) that the Schedule API exposes. If you need to halt a Cron Job for maintenance, you have no clean mechanism; with a Schedule you simply call `Pause()`. A is wrong — Cron Jobs do support arbitrary cron expressions. C is wrong — no dedicated Worker is required. D is fabricated; both mechanisms rely on the Temporal Service for timing.

questions.question: Your team runs a nightly data pipeline using a Temporal Schedule with an interval of 24 hours. During a weekend incident, the worker pool was completely down for 36 hours. When workers come back online, you want the pipeline to execute for each missed interval during the outage. Which backfill approach and overlap policy should you use, and what behavior should you expect from the already-running Schedule?

choices.text: Call TriggerAsync() repeatedly once per missed interval using the default overlap policy; the Schedule will automatically detect the gap and replay missed runs in sequence without any additional configuration.

Call BackfillAsync() with a start/end time covering the outage window and set the overlap policy to AllowAll; this causes Temporal to spin up workflow executions for each interval that would have fired during that period, even if they run concurrently.
Delete the Schedule and recreate it with a new start time; Temporal Schedules cannot recover missed intervals after a worker outage so backfill is not applicable in this scenario.
Call UpdateAsync() to shift the Schedule's interval start time backward by 36 hours; this causes Temporal to retroactively execute the missed intervals before resuming the normal cadence.

questions.explanation: BackfillAsync() (Go: Backfill()) is specifically designed for this scenario. You provide a start and end time range representing the missed window, and Temporal will execute the workflow action for each interval that would have fired during that range. Setting the overlap policy to AllowAll is critical here — without it, if any previously triggered run is still in progress when the next backfill slot fires, the default policy may skip it. Trigger() only fires a single immediate execution and has no concept of a time range. Deleting and recreating the Schedule discards history and doesn't recover missed runs. Updating the interval start time changes future scheduling, not past missed executions.

questions.question: An ops engineer pauses a Temporal Schedule during a database maintenance window, then restarts workers after maintenance completes. She forgets to unpause the Schedule. Three days later, a downstream team reports that no workflow executions have run. In the .NET SDK, which call would she have used to resume the Schedule, and what does pausing actually guarantee about in-flight workflow executions?

choices.text: She should call DeleteAsync() and recreate the Schedule; pausing in Temporal terminates all currently running workflow executions associated with the Schedule, so any in-flight work from before the pause has already been cancelled.

She should call TriggerAsync() with a custom overlap policy; pausing only affects the overlap policy evaluation, so in-flight workflow executions continue normally but the Schedule will not fire new ones until TriggerAsync is called.
She should call UnpauseAsync() on the ScheduleHandle; pausing halts only future workflow runs, so any execution already started before the pause continues running to completion unaffected.

She should call PauseAsync() again with a toggle flag set to false; the PauseAsync() method in .NET acts as a toggle, and pausing stops future runs while also sending a cancellation signal to all currently running workflow executions.

questions.explanation: The correct call to resume a paused Schedule in Go is Unpause() on ScheduleHandle; in .NET the equivalent is calling UnpauseAsync() (or in Go, Unpause()). Critically, pausing a Schedule only stops *future* workflow runs from being initiated — it does not affect workflow executions that are already in progress. Those continue running to completion. This is an important operational distinction: pausing is not a kill switch for running workflows. The .NET SDK does not use PauseAsync() as a toggle; pause and unpause are separate operations. DeleteAsync() would destroy the Schedule entirely.

questions.question: A platform team migrates from Temporal Cron Jobs (CronSchedule field on StartWorkflowOptions) to Temporal Schedules. A developer argues they are functionally equivalent and the migration is just cosmetic. Which operational capability available in Schedules but absent in Cron Jobs most directly disproves this claim?

choices.text: Schedules support cron-style time expressions like '15 8 * * *' while Cron Jobs only accept interval-based durations, so Schedules provide strictly more expressive time specifications.

Schedules can be paused, updated, backfilled, and triggered independently of starting a new workflow execution, whereas Cron Jobs are immutable once created and require spawning a new workflow to change their behavior.
Schedules execute workflow actions faster because the Temporal Service optimizes their task queue routing, whereas Cron Jobs introduce additional scheduling latency due to legacy code paths.
Schedules allow multiple task queues per schedule while Cron Jobs are limited to a single task queue, making Schedules necessary for any multi-worker deployment.

questions.explanation: The Temporal documentation explicitly warns that Cron Jobs are not recommended and that Schedules 'provide a better developer experience, including more configuration options and the ability to update or pause running Schedules.' With Cron Jobs, the cron string is baked into the workflow start options and cannot be changed without terminating and restarting the workflow. Schedules expose UpdateAsync/Update, PauseAsync/Pause, BackfillAsync/Backfill, and TriggerAsync/Trigger as first-class operations on a ScheduleHandle — none of which exist for Cron Jobs. Both Schedules and Cron Jobs support cron expressions, so option A is incorrect. Options C and D describe non-existent technical differences.

questions.question: A team creates a Temporal Schedule that fires once per day for exactly 7 days to run a weekly report series. After the 7th execution completes, a developer notices the Schedule no longer appears in ListSchedulesAsync() results and suspects a bug in their cleanup code. What is the most likely explanation?

choices.text: ListSchedulesAsync() has eventual consistency: schedules added or deleted may not appear immediately, so the Schedule still exists but the list view hasn't caught up yet after all 7 runs completed.

The Schedule was automatically deleted by the Temporal Service after it completed creating all its workflow executions, since it will never fire again; this is expected behavior documented as Schedule Auto-Deletion.
Temporal Schedules expire after a default TTL of 7 days unless a custom retention period is set in ScheduleOptions, so a 7-day schedule is always removed on the 8th day regardless of execution count.

The Schedule was garbage-collected because no workflow executions from it are currently running; Temporal links Schedule lifetime to active workflow executions and purges Schedules with no running children.

questions.explanation: The documentation includes an explicit tip: 'Once a Schedule has completed creating all its Workflow Executions, the Temporal Service deletes it since it won't fire again. The Temporal Service doesn't guarantee when this removal will happen.' This is Schedule Auto-Deletion — a built-in behavior, not a bug. The developer's cleanup code is irrelevant. Option A is partially true (ListSchedulesAsync has eventual consistency for additions/deletions), but that describes a transient lag, not a permanent disappearance. There is no default 7-day TTL for Schedules, and Schedule lifetime is not tied to whether child workflow executions are currently running.

questions.question: In a Go service, a developer uses UpdateAsync() on a ScheduleHandle to change the workflow action — swapping the target workflow function from ReportV1 to ReportV2. She notices that UpdateAsync() takes a callback rather than accepting a new Schedule object directly. A colleague suggests she should instead delete and recreate the Schedule to ensure atomicity. What is the actual purpose of the callback pattern in Schedule Update, and why does it matter in production?

choices.text: The callback pattern is a convenience wrapper that constructs the updated Schedule from scratch; it is functionally identical to delete-and-recreate but avoids the risk of losing the Schedule ID, which is why the colleague's suggestion is discouraged.

The callback receives the current Schedule description as input, allowing the update to be based on the live server-side state rather than a stale local copy; this prevents lost-update races where two concurrent updates could overwrite each other's changes.

The callback defers execution of the update to the next Schedule firing interval, ensuring that the running workflow execution completes before the new action takes effect, which guarantees exactly-once semantics during transitions.

The callback pattern is required because Temporal validates the updated Schedule against the original before persisting it; returning a different Schedule type from the callback causes the update to be rejected with a validation error.

questions.explanation: In both Go and .NET, the UpdateAsync/Update method takes a callback that receives a ScheduleUpdateInput containing the current description of the Schedule as it exists on the server. The caller then returns a modified Schedule from that callback. This read-modify-write pattern is intentional: it ensures the update is derived from the current server state, not from a potentially stale in-memory object. This prevents a class of lost-update bugs common in distributed systems where two operators update the Schedule simultaneously. Delete-and-recreate would lose schedule history, past run information, and potentially create a gap window during which the Schedule doesn't exist — not equivalent to an atomic update.

questions.question: A team wants to run a one-time data migration workflow exactly 6 hours from now, at a specific wall-clock time, without setting up a recurring Schedule. They are debating between creating a Schedule with a single-firing Spec versus using StartDelay on WorkflowOptions. Which approach is correct for a true one-time delayed start, and what does the chosen mechanism actually do differently from the other?

choices.text: Use a Schedule with a CalendarSpec set to the exact target datetime; StartDelay only accepts relative durations and cannot target a specific wall-clock time, making it unsuitable for calendar-aligned one-time executions.

Use StartDelay on WorkflowOptions in StartWorkflowAsync or ExecuteWorkflow; this delays the start of a single workflow execution by a relative duration without creating a Schedule entity, whereas a Schedule creates a persistent managed object with its own lifecycle and ID.
Both approaches are functionally identical for one-time runs; the only difference is that StartDelay has a maximum delay of 24 hours, so for delays longer than that a Schedule is required.

Use StartDelay because Schedules cannot be configured for a single firing — the Spec always requires a repeating interval or cron expression, and a one-time CalendarSpec is not a supported configuration.

questions.explanation: StartDelay is specifically documented as the mechanism to 'schedule a Workflow Execution at a specific one-time future point rather than on a recurring schedule.' It is set on WorkflowOptions and passed to StartWorkflowAsync() or ExecuteWorkflow() — no Schedule entity is created. A Schedule, by contrast, is a persistent server-side object with its own ID, lifecycle, pause/update/backfill capabilities, and auto-deletion semantics. Using a Schedule for a one-time run works but is heavyweight: it creates a managed entity that then gets auto-deleted. StartDelay is the idiomatic lightweight alternative. There is no 24-hour cap documented for StartDelay, and Schedules do support single-firing configurations.

questions.question: An engineer triggers a Scheduled Workflow manually using TriggerAsync() during off-hours testing, but the Schedule has an overlap policy of Skip (the default). A workflow from the previous scheduled interval is still running. What happens to the manually triggered execution, and what change would allow both the scheduled and the manually triggered run to execute concurrently?

choices.text: The manually triggered execution is queued and will start immediately after the currently running workflow completes; to allow concurrent runs, set the overlap policy to BufferAll on the ScheduleHandle's TriggerOptions.

The manually triggered execution is rejected with an error because TriggerAsync() cannot override the Schedule's overlap policy; to allow it, the engineer must pause the running workflow first using the workflow's handle.

The manually triggered execution is skipped because the overlap policy applies to TriggerAsync() by default; to allow concurrent execution, pass an overlap policy of AllowAll in the ScheduleTriggerOptions when calling TriggerAsync().

The manually triggered execution always runs regardless of the overlap policy because TriggerAsync() is an administrative override that bypasses schedule policies; concurrent runs are therefore always possible via manual trigger.

questions.explanation: The documentation states that trigger 'is subject to the Overlap Policy of the Schedule' by default. If the current overlap policy is Skip and a workflow is already running, the triggered execution will be skipped — not queued, not run, simply dropped. However, TriggerAsync() (and its Go equivalent Trigger()) accepts a ScheduleTriggerOptions parameter where you can specify an explicit overlap policy for just that trigger call, overriding the Schedule's default for that single invocation. The Go sample code shows this exact pattern: passing enums.SCHEDULE_OVERLAP_POLICY_ALLOW_ALL in ScheduleTriggerOptions. This is not an administrative override that always bypasses policy — you must explicitly set AllowAll in the options.

questions.question: A platform team uses ListSchedulesAsync() to build a dashboard showing all active Schedules across a namespace. During a deploy, they create 10 new Schedules and immediately query the list, but only 7 appear. Meanwhile, an operator deletes 3 old Schedules but they still show up in the list. After a few seconds, the counts stabilize correctly. What Temporal design characteristic explains this behavior, and what operational implication does it have for code that reads from this list?

choices.text: ListSchedulesAsync() uses a read-your-writes consistency model within the same client connection, but cross-client reads are eventually consistent; the fix is to use the same client instance for both creates and list reads.

The Temporal visibility store that backs list operations is eventually consistent; schedule additions and deletions may not be immediately reflected in list results, so code that depends on list accuracy immediately after a mutation must account for propagation delay.
ListSchedulesAsync() paginates results and the missing schedules are on a subsequent page; callers must set a sufficiently large PageSize or iterate all pages to get a complete count of Schedules.
Newly created Schedules are held in a pending state for up to 30 seconds before becoming visible; this is a deliberate rate-limiting mechanism to prevent Schedule storms during bulk deployments.

questions.explanation: The .NET documentation explicitly warns: 'If a schedule is added or deleted, it may not be available in the list immediately.' This is because ListSchedulesAsync() queries the Temporal visibility store (backed by Elasticsearch or a SQL database depending on deployment), which is an eventually consistent read model. Writes (create, delete) go to the Temporal history service but propagate to the visibility index asynchronously. The operational implication is significant: any code path that creates a Schedule and then immediately checks the list to confirm its existence (or waits for a deletion to clear before proceeding) may get stale results. Engineers should use DescribeAsync() on a known Schedule ID for authoritative existence checks rather than relying on list results immediately after mutations.

choices.text: `ALLOW_ALL` during backfill means each scheduled interval fires sequentially but waits for the previous to finish, so no duplicate runs occur; `SKIP` would be safer to prevent future overlaps from the normal schedule cadence only.

`ALLOW_ALL` during backfill means all missed intervals within the 72-hour window fire simultaneously without any concurrency control, potentially launching dozens of parallel executions over overlapping data windows; `SKIP` or `BUFFER_ONE` would be safer to ensure at most one execution runs at a time during recovery.

`ALLOW_ALL` during backfill ignores the schedule spec entirely and fires exactly one execution per backfill call regardless of how many intervals fit in the window; `CANCEL_OTHER` would be safer because it ensures exactly-once semantics at the schedule level.

`ALLOW_ALL` is only applied to the trigger operation and has no effect on backfill; the backfill always fires exactly the number of times the schedule would have fired in that window sequentially, making the overlap policy irrelevant here.

questions.explanation: Backfill in Temporal replays the schedule's firing logic over the specified historical time window. If the schedule fires every hour and you backfill 72 hours, the server will evaluate 72 nominal firing times and apply the specified overlap policy to each. With `SCHEDULE_OVERLAP_POLICY_ALLOW_ALL`, every one of those 72 firings launches a new workflow execution concurrently — there is no sequencing or throttling. For an idempotent workflow this is fine and often desirable (parallel recovery is fast). For a non-idempotent financial workflow, this is catastrophic: you get up to 72 simultaneous runs all processing potentially overlapping data windows at once. The correct policy for sequential recovery of non-idempotent work is `SKIP` (only runs if nothing is currently running — each newly spawned execution runs to completion before the next backfill-triggered one can start) or `BUFFER_ONE` (queues one pending execution). This interaction — backfill volume × overlap policy × workflow idempotency — is one of the most dangerous production footguns in Temporal Schedules. Engineers should always ask: 'If backfill fires N times simultaneously, does my workflow handle that correctly?' before choosing `ALLOW_ALL`.

questions.question:
A .NET team is migrating from legacy cron jobs to Temporal Schedules. They need a one-time workflow run 3 hours from now (not recurring). A junior engineer proposes creating a Schedule with a single interval of 3 hours and then deleting the schedule after it fires. A senior engineer suggests using `StartDelay` instead. In the context of Temporal's Schedule auto-deletion behavior and the `StartDelay` option, which statement best explains why `StartDelay` is the more correct approach?

A) `StartDelay` on `WorkflowOptions` delays the first workflow task dispatch but keeps the workflow in a `Running` state immediately, whereas a Schedule with a 3-hour interval would also keep a persistent Schedule entity and fire repeatedly every 3 hours unless explicitly deleted after the first firing.
B) `StartDelay` triggers a one-time future execution without creating any Schedule entity, avoiding the overhead of schedule polling infrastructure; the Schedule approach requires the engineer to implement delete logic after the first fire or risk repeated 3-hour interval firings, and the auto-deletion tip only applies when the spec exhausts all possible firing times, not recurring intervals.
C) `StartDelay` is equivalent to a Schedule with a one-time calendar spec, so both approaches are architecturally identical; the only difference is that `StartDelay` offers slightly better UI visibility in the Temporal Web UI.
D) The Schedule auto-deletion feature will automatically remove the 3-hour-interval Schedule after its first firing, making both approaches equivalent in practice; the senior engineer's preference for `StartDelay` is purely stylistic.

choices.text: `StartDelay` on `WorkflowOptions` delays the first workflow task dispatch but keeps the workflow in a `Running` state immediately, whereas a Schedule with a 3-hour interval would also keep a persistent Schedule entity and fire repeatedly every 3 hours unless explicitly deleted after the first firing.

`StartDelay` triggers a one-time future execution without creating any Schedule entity, avoiding the overhead of schedule polling infrastructure; the Schedule approach requires the engineer to implement delete logic after the first fire or risk repeated 3-hour interval firings, and the auto-deletion tip only applies when the spec exhausts all possible firing times, not recurring intervals.

`StartDelay` is equivalent to a Schedule with a one-time calendar spec, so both approaches are architecturally identical; the only difference is that `StartDelay` offers slightly better UI visibility in the Temporal Web UI.

The Schedule auto-deletion feature will automatically remove the 3-hour-interval Schedule after its first firing, making both approaches equivalent in practice; the senior engineer's preference for `StartDelay` is purely stylistic.

questions.explanation: This question tests understanding of two distinct Temporal features for future-dated execution and how they interact with Schedule lifecycle. `StartDelay` is a `WorkflowOptions` property on `StartWorkflowAsync`/`ExecuteWorkflowAsync` that simply delays when the first workflow task is dispatched — it creates a single workflow execution, no Schedule entity, and no recurring infrastructure. The Schedule auto-deletion note in the documentation is specifically scoped to Schedules that 'have completed creating all their Workflow Executions' — meaning a spec that has a defined end time or a limited number of firings that have all been exhausted. A Schedule with `Intervals = [5 days]` or `Intervals = [3 hours]` has no natural end and will fire indefinitely, so auto-deletion will never trigger. If the engineer creates a 3-hour interval Schedule and forgets to delete it, it will keep launching new workflow executions every 3 hours forever. `StartDelay` avoids all of this: no Schedule entity to manage, no recurring behavior, no cleanup burden. Understanding when to use `StartDelay` vs. a one-shot Schedule vs. a recurring Schedule is a fundamental design decision that affects operational complexity, observability, and correctness.

choices.text: `Update()` uses an optimistic concurrency control mechanism: the callback receives the current schedule description including a conflict token, and if the server detects a concurrent modification between when the description was read and when the update is applied, it retries the callback with a fresh description automatically.

`Update()` acquires a distributed lock on the schedule entity for the duration of the RPC call, so concurrent calls from multiple goroutines are serialized by the server; the risk is only that one goroutine's update overwrites another's if both read and modify the same field simultaneously in the callback.
`Update()` has no concurrency protection; the last writer wins without any conflict detection, meaning both goroutines can overwrite each other's changes silently without error or retry.

`Update()` uses a two-phase commit protocol across the frontend and matching services; concurrent updates will cause one call to return a `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` error that the SDK automatically retries with exponential backoff.

questions.explanation: Temporal's Schedule `Update()` API uses an optimistic concurrency control (OCC) pattern. The callback passed to `Update()` / `UpdateAsync()` receives an `input` object containing the current schedule description as read from the server at that moment. The SDK sends this description back along with a conflict token derived from the server-side version. If a concurrent update has modified the schedule between the read and the write, the server detects the conflict token mismatch and the SDK automatically re-invokes the callback with the freshened description and retries the update. This is the same OCC pattern used throughout Temporal for workflow updates, signal handling, and visibility writes — it avoids distributed locks (which are fragile and block progress) in favor of retry-on-conflict semantics. The risk with concurrent goroutines calling `Update()` is not data corruption (OCC handles that) but rather livelock or high retry rates if the contention is severe, and logical issues if each goroutine's callback makes decisions based on stale assumptions about what fields were changed by the other. Engineers running concurrent schedule management code should serialize updates at the application layer or ensure callbacks are idempotent with respect to concurrent modifications.
